questions.question: What bottleneck do swarm architectures eliminate, and at what cost?
options: GPU memory limits, at the cost of accuracy
The central supervisor (every decision funnels through one agent; one slow plan step stalls everything) — traded for loss of determinism and traceability
Network latency, at the cost of security
Token costs, at the cost of speed
questions.explanation: Workers pull from a shared queue instead of waiting for dispatch; coordination is baked into event-bus semantics. The system scales until the queue does — and debugging asynchronous, log-scattered runs becomes the new expensive thing.

questions.question: Which task profile fits a swarm, and which does not?
options: Fits: anything with multiple agents; doesn't fit: single-agent tasks
Fits: many independent tasks with variable durations where throughput matters; doesn't fit: ordered workflows and global-plan tasks needing one coherent result
Fits: small tasks only; doesn't fit: large tasks
Swarms fit every workload better than supervisors
questions.explanation: Scraping/transforming/classifying with automatic load balancing is the sweet spot. Step-3-needs-step-2 workflows risk firing out of order, and a swarm of researchers produces independent facts, not a coherent report.

questions.question: What is Matrix's (arXiv:2511.21686) core architectural move?
options: A matrix-multiplication accelerator for agents
Both control flow AND data flow become serialized messages on distributed queues — no central coordinator; fault tolerance comes from message durability, scalability from the broker
A single supervisor with matrix-organized workers
Embedding agents inside a database
questions.explanation: Coordination becomes "which topic does this agent subscribe to?" rather than "who does the supervisor pick next?" — a pub/sub event mesh where the orchestrator bottleneck simply doesn't exist.

questions.question: What is queue starvation in a swarm, and how is it mitigated?
options: Workers running out of RAM; add more memory
Workers all pull the fastest tasks so long tasks wait until last — mitigated by priority aging, worker specialization for long tasks, and back-pressure on fast-task inflow
The queue server crashing; add replicas
Agents refusing tasks; raise their incentives
questions.explanation: Classic queue pathology, inherited intact by agent swarms. Aging raises priority with wait time; dedicating some workers to long tasks and limiting fast-task entry keep the tail from starving.

questions.question: How does content-based routing extend the basic swarm?
options: It compresses message contents
One queue per message type with specialist workers subscribing only to their type — the basis of message-bus architectures that scale to thousands of agents
It routes based on message length
It encrypts queues per worker
questions.explanation: The generic shared queue becomes a typed topic mesh. Specialists pull only what they can handle, which is how swarms grow from a handful of workers into large-scale agent meshes.
